annotations.label: Fractal

anchor.snippet: a fractal is a geometric shape containing detailed structure at arbitrarily small scales

annotations.note: Mathlib has no general definition of a 'fractal' as a geometric object.

annotations.label: Self-similarity

anchor.snippet: This exhibition of similar patterns at increasingly smaller scales is called self-similarity

annotations.note: No notion of self-similarity (or self-similar set) is defined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Affine self-similar

anchor.snippet: the shape is called affine self-similar

annotations.note: Affine self-similarity is not formalized; Mathlib has affine maps but no self-similarity predicate.

annotations.label: Fractal scaling by non-integer power

anchor.snippet: the spatial content of the fractal scales by a power that is not necessarily an integer

annotations.note: `hausdorffMeasure_smul₀` proves μH[d](r•s)=‖r‖^d•μH[d] s for real d, formalizing how d-dimensional content scales by a possibly non-integer power, but without the 'fractal' framing.

annotations.label: Fractal dimension

anchor.snippet: This power is called the fractal dimension of the geometric object

annotations.note: `MeasureTheory.dimH` is the Hausdorff dimension, the rigorous form of the fractal/scaling dimension defined for arbitrary sets.

annotations.label: Fractals nowhere differentiable

anchor.snippet: many fractals are nowhere differentiable

annotations.note: This informal claim about fractals is not a Mathlib theorem (the concrete Weierstrass example exists only in the Counterexamples directory).

annotations.label: Mandelbrot 1982 definition

anchor.snippet: A fractal is by definition a set for which the Hausdorff–Besicovitch dimension strictly exceeds the topological dimension

annotations.note: Hausdorff dimension (`dimH`) and a topological dimension (`smallInductiveDimension`) are both formalized, but the fractal definition comparing them is not.

annotations.label: Mandelbrot expanded definition

anchor.snippet: A fractal is a rough or fragmented geometric shape that can be split into parts

annotations.note: This informal descriptive definition has no Mathlib counterpart.

annotations.label: Rep-tiling of a line

anchor.snippet: if such a figure is rep-tiled into pieces each 1/3 the length of the original, then there are always three equal pieces

annotations.note: The linear scaling of 1-dimensional Hausdorff measure under a scale factor is formalized, but rep-tiling of a segment into congruent copies is not.

annotations.label: Rep-tiling of a square

anchor.snippet: A solid square is understood to be two-dimensional

annotations.note: A square's dimension being 2 follows from `dimH_univ_eq_finrank` (and area scales by r² via `hausdorffMeasure_smul₀`), but rep-tiling is not formalized.

annotations.label: Rep-tiling rule for n-dimensional objects

anchor.snippet: being n-dimensional means that when it is rep-tiled into pieces each scaled down by a scale-factor of

annotations.note: The scaling law μH[n](r•s)=r^n·μH[n] s captures the n-dimensional scaling content, but the rep-tiling/piece-counting formulation is absent.

annotations.label: Fractal dimension of the Koch curve

anchor.snippet: the unique real number D that satisfies

annotations.note: The Koch curve and its Hausdorff dimension log 4/log 3 are not formalized (Snowflaking.lean only mentions the Koch snowflake in prose).

annotations.label: Koch snowflake has infinite perimeter

anchor.snippet: the snowflake has an infinite perimeter

annotations.note: The Koch snowflake is not defined in Mathlib, so its infinite perimeter is not formalized.

annotations.label: Weierstrass function

anchor.snippet: the non- intuitive property of being everywhere continuous but nowhere differentiable

annotations.note: A continuous-everywhere, nowhere-differentiable Weierstrass function is fully formalized, though it lives in the Counterexamples directory rather than the core Mathlib library.

annotations.label: Cantor sets

anchor.snippet: examples of subsets of the real line known as Cantor sets

annotations.note: `cantorSet` is the ternary Cantor set defined as ⋂ₙ preCantorSet n on the real line.

annotations.label: Non-integer (Hausdorff) dimension

anchor.snippet: to allow for sets to have non-integer dimensions

annotations.note: `MeasureTheory.dimH` takes values in ℝ≥0∞ and may be non-integer, exactly the Hausdorff dimension allowing fractional dimensions.

annotations.label: Hausdorff–Besicovitch dimension exceeds topological

anchor.snippet: to denote an object whose Hausdorff–Besicovitch dimension is greater than its topological dimension

annotations.note: Both `dimH` and a topological (`smallInductiveDimension`) dimension exist, but Mathlib has no predicate for dimH exceeding topological dimension.

annotations.label: Hilbert curve counterexample

anchor.snippet: this requirement is not met by space-filling curves such as the Hilbert curve

annotations.note: A space-filling (Peano) curve from [0,1] onto the square is formalized in Counterexamples, but the Hilbert curve specifically and the dimension-counterexample claim are not.

annotations.label: Exact self-similarity

anchor.snippet: Exact self-similarity: identical at all scales, such as the Koch snowflake

annotations.note: Exact self-similarity is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Quasi self-similarity

anchor.snippet: Quasi self-similarity: approximates the same pattern at different scales

annotations.note: Quasi self-similarity has no Mathlib formalization.

annotations.label: Statistical self-similarity

anchor.snippet: Statistical self-similarity: repeats a pattern stochastically so numerical or statistical measures are preserved across scales

annotations.note: Statistical self-similarity is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Qualitative self-similarity

anchor.snippet: Qualitative self-similarity: as in a time series

annotations.note: Qualitative self-similarity is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Multifractal scaling

anchor.snippet: Multifractal scaling: characterized by more than one fractal dimension or scaling rule

annotations.note: Multifractal scaling and multifractal spectra are not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Fine structure at small scales

anchor.snippet: Fine or detailed structure at arbitrarily small scales

annotations.note: This qualitative fractal characteristic has no Mathlib counterpart.

annotations.label: Irregularity

anchor.snippet: Irregularity locally and globally that cannot easily be described in the language of traditional Euclidean geometry

annotations.note: This qualitative fractal characteristic is not formalized.

annotations.label: Straight line is not a fractal

anchor.snippet: A straight line, for instance, is self-similar but not fractal because it lacks detail

annotations.note: dimH and finrank of a line both equal 1 in Mathlib, but 'fractal' is undefined so this negative claim cannot be stated.

annotations.label: Iterated function systems

anchor.snippet: Iterated function systems (IFS) – use fixed geometric replacement rules

annotations.note: Iterated function systems and their attractors are not formalized (only the underlying Banach fixed-point infrastructure exists).

annotations.label: Strange attractors

anchor.snippet: Strange attractors – use iterations of a map or solutions of a system of initial-value differential or difference equations that exhibit chaos

annotations.note: Strange attractors and chaotic dynamics are not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: L-systems

anchor.snippet: L-systems – use string rewriting

annotations.note: Lindenmayer systems / string rewriting for fractals are not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Escape-time fractals

anchor.snippet: Escape-time fractals – use a formula or recurrence relation at each point in a space

annotations.note: Escape-time fractals (e.g. the Mandelbrot/Julia sets) are not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Random fractals

anchor.snippet: Random fractals – use stochastic rules

annotations.note: Random/stochastic fractals are not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Finite subdivision rules

anchor.snippet: Finite subdivision rules – use a recursive topological algorithm for refining tilings

annotations.note: Finite subdivision rules for refining tilings are not formalized in Mathlib.
